signs: Unconscious or unresponsive after water submersion
dos: Call for emergency help immediately
Remove person from water safely
Begin CPR immediately if not breathing — 30 chest compressions then 2 rescue breaths
Keep them warm with a blanket after rescue
Place in recovery position if breathing returns
donts: Do NOT waste time trying to drain water before CPR
Do NOT leave them alone even if conscious
Do NOT give food or drink
urgent: All drowning victims need emergency care even if they seem fine — delayed complications can be fatal.

signs: Pain, redness or swelling in the eye
Sudden loss of vision or blurred vision
dos: For chemical splash: flush with clean water for 15–20 minutes continuously
For foreign object: blink rapidly to try to dislodge it naturally
Cover the eye loosely with a clean cloth
Keep the person calm and still
donts: Do NOT rub the eye
Do NOT try to remove an embedded object
Do NOT use cotton wool directly on the eyeball
Do NOT delay seeking care for any eye injury
urgent: All serious eye injuries require urgent medical care. Vision loss can be permanent if treatment is delayed.

signs: Sudden swelling of face, lips, or throat
Skin rash, hives, or extreme itching
dos: If prescribed: use epinephrine (EpiPen) immediately
Lay person flat with legs raised (unless breathing is difficult)
Call for emergency help immediately for severe reactions
Keep them calm and still
Loosen tight clothing
donts: Do NOT give food or drink
Do NOT leave them alone
Do NOT assume mild reactions won't worsen — they can rapidly
urgent: Anaphylaxis (severe allergic reaction) is life-threatening. Seek emergency care immediately even if symptoms seem to improve.

signs: Sudden confusion, shakiness, or sweating (low blood sugar)
Sweet or fruity breath odor (high blood sugar)
dos: If conscious and low blood sugar suspected: give sugary drink, juice, or glucose tablets
Have them rest and check blood sugar if meter available
If unconscious: place in recovery position, do not give anything by mouth
Call for help immediately if unconscious
donts: Do NOT give sugar if person is unconscious — choking risk
Do NOT leave them alone
Do NOT assume it will resolve on its own if unconscious
urgent: Seek urgent care if person is unconscious, confused, or does not improve within 15 minutes of sugar intake.

signs: Pressure, squeezing, or tightness in chest
Pain spreading to arm, jaw, neck, or back
dos: Call for emergency help immediately
Have person sit or lie in most comfortable position
Loosen tight clothing
If aspirin available and person not allergic: give one adult aspirin (325mg) to chew
Begin CPR if person becomes unresponsive and not breathing normally
donts: Do NOT leave the person alone
Do NOT let them walk around or exert themselves
Do NOT give aspirin if allergic or if stomach bleeding is suspected
urgent: Any chest pain is a potential heart attack. Treat as emergency and seek immediate care — time is critical.

dos: Keep person still — do not move if spinal injury possible
Monitor breathing and consciousness continuously
Apply gentle pressure with clean cloth if bleeding from scalp
Keep awake and talking if conscious
Place in recovery position if unconscious and breathing
donts: Do NOT remove helmet if wearing one (let medical staff do it)
Do NOT give pain medication without medical advice
Do NOT let them sleep unmonitored for first few hours
Do NOT allow return to activity even if they feel fine
urgent: Any loss of consciousness, worsening headache, vomiting, or confusion after head injury requires immediate emergency care.

signs: Nausea, vomiting, or stomach cramps after ingesting unknown substance
dos: Call poison control or emergency services immediately
Keep the person calm and still
Note what was ingested, when, and how much if known
If chemical on skin: remove clothing and rinse skin with water for 20 minutes
Keep any containers or packaging to show medical staff
donts: Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by poison control
Do NOT give milk or water unless advised
Do NOT wait for symptoms to worsen before seeking help
urgent: All suspected poisonings require immediate medical attention. Do not wait.

signs: Deformity or unnatural angle of limb
Inability to move the injured area
dos: Immobilize the injured area — do not try to straighten it
Make a splint using stiff material (stick, rolled newspaper) padded with cloth
Apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling
Elevate if possible
Seek medical care — all fractures need professional treatment
donts: Do NOT try to realign the bone
Do NOT apply tight bandages that cut off circulation
Do NOT move an injured spine — call for help
Do NOT walk on a suspected broken leg or foot
urgent: Open fractures (bone through skin), suspected spine injuries, and any fracture with loss of sensation require immediate emergency care.
